steps.title: Separate style from identity
steps.content: The passage contrasts two kinds of Indigenous abstraction. Contemporary painters such as Mara White Crane may use motifs from beadwork and other traditional arts, so the Indigenous connection can appear in the style itself. Sandoval's generation instead aligned with Abstract Expressionism, a school dominated by European American artists, rather than with traditionally nonrepresentational Indigenous art.

steps.title: Reject added claims
steps.content:
• Choice A - 'place a greater emphasis on the artist's biography than on the aesthetic merit of the painting.': The passage is about how a painting is identified as Indigenous art, not about valuing biography over aesthetic merit.
  • Choice B - 'obscure the Indigenous origins of certain motifs associated with Abstract Expressionism.': The passage does not say Abstract Expressionist motifs have Indigenous origins. It says Sandoval's generation used Abstract Expressionist strategies instead of traditional Indigenous visual forms.

steps.title: Identify what signals remain
steps.content:
• Choice D - 'deny the extent to which cultural identity influences an artist's work.': The passage does not deny cultural influence; it says that influence is not necessarily visible in the style of Sandoval's generation.
  • Choice C - 'depend not on stylistic details but instead on an awareness of the artist's identity.': If the style resembles Abstract Expressionism rather than traditional Indigenous forms, viewers must know the artist's identity to identify the painting as Indigenous art. The answer is C.
